name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّمَا يُؤْمِنُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهَا خَرُّوا۟ سُجَّدًا وَسَبَّحُوا۟ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ ۩ (١٥)
```

```english
Only those believe in Our signs who, when they are reminded of them, fall down in prostration and celebrate the praise of their Lord, and they are not arrogant. (15)
```

```arabic
تَتَجَافَىٰ جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَضَاجِعِ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (١٦)
```

```english
Their sides vacate their beds to supplicate their Lord in fear and hope, and they spend out of what We have provided them. (16)
```

```arabic
فَلَا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ مَّآ أُخْفِىَ لَهُم مِّن قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
No one knows what delights have been kept hidden for them [in the Hereafter] as a reward for what they used to do. (17)
```

```arabic
أَفَمَن كَانَ مُؤْمِنًا كَمَن كَانَ فَاسِقًا ۚ لَّا يَسْتَوُۥنَ (١٨)
```

```english
Is someone who is faithful like someone who is a transgressor? They are not equal. (18)
```

```arabic
أَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَلَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰ نُزُلًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be the gardens of the Abode—a hospitality for what they used to do. (19)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فَسَقُوا۟ فَمَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أَرَادُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَآ أُعِيدُوا۟ فِيهَا وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
As for those who have transgressed, their refuge will be the Fire. Whenever they seek to leave it, they will be turned back into it and told: ‘Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny.’ (20)
```

```arabic
وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْأَدْنَىٰ دُونَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْأَكْبَرِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
We shall surely make them taste the nearer punishment prior to the greater punishment, so that they may come back. (21)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن ذُكِّرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ ثُمَّ أَعْرَضَ عَنْهَآ ۚ إِنَّا مِنَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ مُنتَقِمُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who is reminded of his Lord’s signs, whereat he disregards them? Indeed, We shall take vengeance upon the guilty. (22)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَلَا تَكُن فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّن لِّقَآئِهِۦ ۖ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هُدًى لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (٢٣)
```

```english
Certainly We gave Moses the Book, [declaring], ‘Do not be in doubt about the encounter with Him,’ and We made it a [source of] guidance for the Children of Israel. (23)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا۟ ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يُوقِنُونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
When they had been patient and had conviction in Our signs, We appointed amongst them imams to guide [the people] by Our command. (24)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (25)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ يَمْشُونَ فِى مَسَـٰكِنِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ ۖ أَفَلَا يَسْمَعُونَ (٢٦)
```

```english
Does it not dawn upon them how many generations We have destroyed before them, amid [the ruins of] whose dwellings they walk? There are indeed signs in that. Will they not then listen? (26)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا نَسُوقُ ٱلْمَآءَ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْجُرُزِ فَنُخْرِجُ بِهِۦ زَرْعًا تَأْكُلُ مِنْهُ أَنْعَـٰمُهُمْ وَأَنفُسُهُمْ ۖ أَفَلَا يُبْصِرُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
Do they not see that We carry water to the parched earth and with it We bring forth crops, from which they eat, themselves and their cattle? Will they not then see? (27)
```

```arabic
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْفَتْحُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٨)
```

```english
They say, ‘When will this judgement be, if you are truthful?’ (28)
```

```arabic
قُلْ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَتْحِ لَا يَنفَعُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنُهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٢٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘On the day of judgement their [newly found] faith will not avail the faithless, nor will they be granted any respite.’ (29)
```

```arabic
فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَٱنتَظِرْ إِنَّهُم مُّنتَظِرُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
So turn away from them, and wait. They too are waiting. (30)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses distinguish between the believers (المؤمنين) — *those who possess the true essence of faith* — and the transgressors (الفاسقين) and the wrongdoers (الظالمين). They mention the necessary effects and consequences for each group, then warn the wrongdoers of the punishment of this world (عذاب الدنيا), and command the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to await the victory (الفتح). At that point, the Surah concludes.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّما يُؤْمِنُ بِآياتِنَا الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِّرُوا بِها خَرُّوا سُجَّداً وَسَبَّحُوا بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ** — "Only those believe in Our signs who, when they are reminded of them, fall down in prostration and celebrate the praise of their Lord, and they are not boastful" (32:15). After mentioning a portion of the discourse regarding the disbelievers (الكفار) who deny the meeting with Him and wax proud in this world against faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), He begins describing those who believe in the signs of their Lord and submit to the truth when they are reminded and exhorted.

His saying: **إِنَّما يُؤْمِنُ بِآياتِنَا** (Only those believe in Our signs) is a restriction (حصر) of faith in its true sense to them. Its meaning is that the sign of readiness for true faith (الإيمان الحقيقي) is such and such.

His saying: **الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِّرُوا بِها خَرُّوا سُجَّداً** (who, when they are reminded of them, fall down in prostration) — the Glorified mentioned some of their attributes and some of their actions. As for their attributes, it is their humility (تذللهم) before the station of Lordship (مقام الربوبية) and their lack of arrogance (عدم استكبارهم) in submitting to Allah, glorifying Him, and praising Him. This is His saying: **إِذا ذُكِّرُوا بِها** (when they are reminded of them) — meaning the signs indicating His oneness in His Lordship and Divinity (ألوهيته), and what follows from that regarding the Return (المعاد) and the prophetic call to faith and righteous deeds — **خَرُّوا سُجَّداً** (they fall down in prostration), meaning they fall to the ground prostrating to Allah in humility and submissiveness (استكانة). **وَسَبَّحُوا بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ** (and celebrate the praise of their Lord), meaning they declare His transcendence (نزهوه) coupled with beautiful praise (الثناء الجميل) of Him. Although prostration, glorification, and praise are actions, they are manifestations of the attribute of humility and submission to the station of Lordship and Divinity. Therefore, He followed them with a concomitant attribute, saying: **وَهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ** (and they are not boastful).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **تَتَجافى جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً وَمِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ** — "Their sides vacate their beds to supplicate their Lord in fear and hope, and they spend out of what We have provided them" (32:16). This identifies them in terms of their actions, just as the previous verse identified them in terms of their attributes.

His saying: **تَتَجافى جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ** (Their sides vacate their beds) — **التجافي** (vacating/shunning) means moving away, and **الجنوب** (sides) is the plural of **جنب** (side/flank), and **المضاجع** (beds) is the plural of **مضجع** (place of lying down/sleeping). Shunning the beds is a metaphor (كناية) for forsaking sleep.

His saying: **يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً** (to supplicate their Lord in fear and hope) is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the pronoun in "their sides." The meaning is their preoccupation with supplicating their Lord in the depth of the night when eyes are asleep and breaths are still. This is not out of fear of His wrath alone, such that they would be overwhelmed by despair (اليأس) of Allah's mercy, nor out of hope for His reward alone, such that they would feel secure from His anger and plan (مكره). Rather, they supplicate Him in both fear and hope, prioritizing the etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية) in their supplication over what their guidance (الهدى) incites them toward. This shunning of beds and supplication applies to the night-supererogatory prayers (النوافل الليلية).

His saying: **وَمِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ** (and they spend out of what We have provided them) is another of their deeds, which is spending for the sake of Allah and in His path.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "No one knows what has been kept hidden for them of bright eyes as a reward for what they used to do" (32:17). This is a consequence (تفريع) of their attributes and deeds, describing the reward Allah has prepared for them.

The occurrence of the word "soul" (نفس) as an indefinite noun (نكرة) in a negative context (سياق النفي) denotes universality (العموم). The annexation of "delight" (قرة) to "eyes" (أعين) — rather than "their eyes" — implies that what is hidden for them is a delight for the eye of every possessor of an eye.

The meaning is: No soul among souls knows — meaning it is beyond their knowledge and conception — what Allah has hidden for them of that which delights the eye of every possessor of an eye, as a reward (جزاء) in exchange for what they used to do in the world.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ مُؤْمِناً كَمَنْ كانَ فاسِقاً لا يَسْتَوُونَ** — "Is he who is a believer like him who is a transgressor? They are not equal" (32:18). **Faith** (الإيمان) is a specific cognitive tranquility (سكون علمي) of the soul regarding a thing, and its necessary consequence is practical commitment (الالتزام العملي) to what one has believed in. **Transgression** (الفسق) is the departure from the aforementioned commitment; it is derived from when a date "transgresses" (فسقت) by emerging from its skin. The ultimate meaning is departing from the garb of servitude (زي العبودية).

The interrogative in the verse is for denial (الإنكار), and His saying: **لا يَسْتَوُونَ** (They are not equal) is a negation of equality between the two groups, serving as an emphasis (تأكيد) of what the preceding denial implies.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَلَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ الْمَأْوى نُزُلاً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be the gardens of the Abode as a hospitality for what they used to do" (32:19). **The Abode** (المأوى) is the place where a person seeks refuge and resides. **The hospitality** (النزل) refers to everything prepared for a guest (النازل) in a house, such as food and drink; then it was generalized, as has been said, to every gift. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فَسَقُوا فَمَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ** (As for those who transgress, their abode is the Fire) to the end of the verse. The fact that the Fire is their abode necessitates their eternity (خلودهم) therein; therefore, He followed it with His saying: **كُلَّما أَرادُوا أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها أُعِيدُوا فِيها** (Whenever they desire to leave it, they will be turned back into it). His saying: **وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ ذُوقُوا عَذابَ النَّارِ الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ** (and they will be told, ‘Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny’) is evidence that those who transgressed are the deniers of the Return (المعاد). Addressing them with this speech while they are in the Fire is a form of gloating (شماتة) over them, as they often used to gloat over the believers in the world for their belief in the Return.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُمْ مِنَ الْعَذابِ الْأَدْنى دُونَ الْعَذابِ الْأَكْبَرِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** — "We shall surely make them taste the nearer punishment prior to the greater punishment, so that they may come back" (32:21). Since the purpose of making them taste the punishment is their hoped-for return, and the hoped-for return is the return to Allah through repentance (التوبة) and contrition (الإنابة), the "nearer punishment" (العذاب الأدنى) refers to the punishment of this world (عذاب الدنيا) that descends upon them for intimidation and warning so that they may repent — excluding the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) and the punishment after death. Consequently, the "greater punishment" (العذاب الأكبر) refers to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection.

The meaning is: I swear We shall surely make them taste the nearer punishment — meaning the closer one, such as years of famine, diseases, being killed, and the like — before the greater punishment on the Day of Resurrection, so that they may return to Us by repenting from their polytheism (شركهم) and denial.

It is said: The punishment of this world is called "nearer" (أدنى) and He did not say "the smaller" (الأصغر) to contrast with "the greater" (الأكبر) because the context is one of warning and intimidation, and it is not suitable to count the punishment as "smaller." Likewise, He did not say "prior to the farther punishment" (العذاب الأبعد) to contrast with the "nearer punishment" because it does not suit the context of intimidation.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ ذُكِّرَ بِآياتِ رَبِّهِ ثُمَّ أَعْرَضَ عَنْها إِنَّا مِنَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُنْتَقِمُونَ** — "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who is reminded of the signs of his Lord, then he turns away from them? Indeed We will take vengeance upon the guilty" (32:22). It is as if this is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for their punishment with the greater punishment, given that they are deniers. He justified it by the fact that they are wrongdoers of the most severe kind by turning away from the signs after being reminded; thus, they are criminals (مجرمين), and Allah takes vengeance (منتقم) upon them.

So His saying: **وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ** (Who is a greater wrongdoer) etc., is a justification for their punishment because they are wrongdoers of the most severe kind. Then His saying: **إِنَّا مِنَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُنْتَقِمُونَ** (Indeed We will take vengeance upon the guilty) is a justification for the punishment of the wrongdoers because they are criminals, and the punishment is a vengeance (انتقام) upon them, and Allah takes vengeance upon the criminals.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ فَلا تَكُنْ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْ لِقائِهِ وَجَعَلْناهُ هُدىً لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ** — "Certainly We gave Moses the Book, so do not be in doubt about the encounter with Him, and We made it a guidance for the Children of Israel" (32:23). The "Book" (الكتاب) refers to the Torah, and **المرية** means doubt and suspicion (الشك والريب).

They have differed regarding the referent of the pronoun in His saying: **مِنْ لِقائِهِ** (about the encounter with Him/it) and the meaning of the word. It is said: The pronoun refers to Moses, and he is the object of the "encounter" (اللقاء); the estimation being: "Do not be in doubt about your meeting Moses," and he indeed met him on the Night of Ascension (ليلة المعراج) as mentioned in the narrations. If the Surah was revealed after the Ascension, it is a reminder of what had occurred; if it was revealed before it, it is a promise from Him, the Exalted, to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he would see him.

It is also said: The pronoun refers to Moses, and the meaning is: "Do not be in doubt about your meeting Moses on the Day of Resurrection."

It is said: The pronoun refers to the Book, and the estimation is: "Do not be in doubt about Moses' receiving (لقاء) the Book."

It is said: The estimation is: "about your receiving the Book" or "about the Book's reaching you."

It is said: The pronoun refers to the harm (الأذى) Moses encountered from his people, and the meaning is: "Do not be in doubt about encountering harm just as Moses encountered it from his people." You are aware that a sound nature (الطبع السليم) does not accept any of these views — moreover, they are insufficient in explaining the connection of the verse to what preceded it.

It is possible — and Allah knows best — that the pronoun in "His encounter" (لقائه) refers to Him, the Exalted. The "encounter with Him" refers to the Resurrection (البعث) by virtue of it being the day they are present before their Lord with no veil between Him and them, as previously mentioned. He expressed it as "encounter" (اللقاء) a few verses prior in His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ** (rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord), then expressed it with what carries its meaning in His saying: **ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** (hanging their heads before their Lord).

Thus, the meaning would be: We certainly gave Moses the Book just as We gave you the Quran, so do not be in doubt about the Resurrection which the Quran speaks of, by doubting the Quran itself. The revelation of the Quran to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has been supported by the revelation of the Torah to Moses in several places in the Quran. This is supported by His subsequent saying: **وَجَعَلْناهُ هُدىً لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَجَعَلْنا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا** (and We made it a guidance for the Children of Israel, and We appointed from among them leaders who guide by Our command) etc.

It is also possible that the "encounter with Him" refers to the total detachment (الانقطاع التام) toward Him, the Exalted, at the time of the revelation of the Quran or part of it, as mentioned in some narrations. This would be a return to what was at the beginning of the Surah in His saying: **تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** (The revelation of the Book, there is no doubt in it, is from the Lord of all the worlds). The end of the verse supports this view more strongly than the previous one, and Allah knows best.

His saying: **وَجَعَلْناهُ هُدىً لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ** (and We made it a guidance for the Children of Israel) — meaning a guide (هادياً); so the verbal noun (المصدر) is used in the sense of the active participle (اسم الفاعل) or in its original sense for emphasis (مبالغة).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَجَعَلْنا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا وَكانُوا بِآياتِنا يُوقِنُونَ** — "And We appointed from among them leaders who guide by Our command, when they had been patient and had conviction in Our signs" (32:24). Meaning: We appointed from the Children of Israel leaders (أئمة) who guide the people by Our command; We only appointed them as guiding leaders for the people when they were patient in the religion, having previously attained certainty (موقنين) in Our signs.

The discussion regarding the meaning of Imamate (الإمامة) and the Imam's guidance by Allah's command has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: **قالَ إِنِّي جاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِماماً** (He said, ‘I am making you an Imam for mankind’) [al-Baqarah: 124], and His saying: **وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا** (We made them leaders who guide by Our command) [al-Anbiya: 73], and other relevant places.

These two verses encompass the widespread mercy (الرحمة المنبسطة) through the Torah: that it is a guidance in itself, guiding whoever follows it to the truth, and that it produced, within the cradle of its upbringing, people whom Allah chose for Imamate, so they began guiding by His command. Thus, it is blessed for acting upon it, and blessed after the action.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ** — "Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ" (32:25). He means their differences in religion, which only occurred out of envy (بغياً) among themselves, as He mentions in various places of His speech, such as His saying: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ** — until He said — **فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ** (Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book... They did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ) [al-Jathiyah: 16-17].

The meaning of His saying: **يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ** (will judge/decide between them) is the decisive judgment (القضاء) that distinguishes between truth and falsehood, and the one on the truth and the one on falsehood. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ يَمْشُونَ فِي مَساكِنِهِمْ** (Does it not guide them [to see] how many generations We have destroyed before them, in whose dwellings they [now] walk?) etc. The conjunction is to something omitted, as if it were said: "Has it not been made clear to them such and such, and **أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ** (does it not guide them) etc." Guidance (الهداية) here is in the sense of clarification (التبيين), or it encompasses the meaning of clarification, which is why it is made transitive with the preposition *lam* (لـ).

His saying: **كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ** (how many generations We have destroyed before them) points to the subject (الفاعل) and stands in its place. The meaning is: Has the large number of generations We destroyed before them not been made clear to them, while they walk in their dwellings?

His saying: **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ أَفَلا يَسْمَعُونَ** (Indeed there are signs in that; do they not then listen?) — the "listening" (السمع) intended is the listening to exhortations that leads to obedience to the truth and its acceptance.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا نَسُوقُ الْماءَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ الْجُرُزِ فَنُخْرِجُ بِهِ زَرْعاً تَأْكُلُ مِنْهُ أَنْعامُهُمْ وَأَنْفُسُهُمْ** (Do they not see that We drive the water to the parched land and We bring forth crops therewith, of which their cattle and they themselves eat?) etc. It is said in *al-Majma‘*: **السوق** (driving) is urging to travel, from *saqahu yasuquhu*.

He said: **الجرز** (parched) is the dry land that has no vegetation due to the cessation of rain. [End quote].

**الزرع** (crops) is originally a verbal noun, and what is meant here is the thing planted (المزروع).

The verse mentions another sign from the signs of Allah, the Glorified, indicating His excellent governance (تدبيره) of things, especially living beings like cattle and humans. The "driving of water to the land devoid of vegetation" refers to driving the clouds carrying rain to it. In the descent of rainwater from them is the life of the earth, the emergence of crops, and the nourishment of humans and the cattle they subjugate and raise for their life's purposes.

His saying: **أَفَلا يُبْصِرُونَ** (Do they not then see?) is an awakening and a rebuke. This sign is specifically associated with "seeing" (الإبصار), while the previous verse was associated with "listening" (السمع), because knowledge of the destruction of past nations is only through reports (الأخبار) obtained via the sense of hearing. As for knowledge of driving rain to parched land, the emergence of crops, and the nourishment of cattle and humans, the path to it is the sense of sight.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْفَتْحُ** — until His saying — **وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ** ("They say, ‘When will this victory be, if you are truthful?’... and they will not be granted any respite" [32:28-29]). Al-Raghib said: **الفتح** (victory/opening) is the removal of closure and ambiguity — until he said — and "opening the case" (فتح القضية) means deciding the matter and removing the closure from it. He said: **رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْفاتِحِينَ** (Our Lord! Judge between us and our people with justice, and You are the best of judges) [7:89]. [End quote].

In the preceding verses, two things occurred that qualify as "victory" (الفتح) in the sense of "decisive judgment" (الفصل): one is the judgment between them on the Day of Resurrection, and the other is making them taste the nearer punishment or taking vengeance upon them in this world. Therefore, some have interpreted "the victory" as the Day of Resurrection; thus, the meaning of their saying: **مَتى هذَا الْفَتْحُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** (When will this victory be, if you are truthful?) is the same as their saying frequently recounted in His speech: **مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** (When will this promise be, if you are truthful?).

Some interpreted it as the Day of Badr, for the faith of the polytheists who were killed did not benefit them after being killed.

Some mentioned that what is meant is the Conquest of Mecca (فتح مكة), but the answer mentioned in His saying: **قُلْ يَوْمَ الْفَتْحِ لا يَنْفَعُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِيمانُهُمْ وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ** (Say, ‘On the day of victory their faith will not benefit the faithless, nor will they be granted any respite’) does not suit this, unless one says: their faith on that day — after they had obstinately opposed the truth, fought the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for years, and struggled to extinguish the light of Allah — was nothing but hypocrisy (نفاق), without entering their hearts or benefiting their souls, and they were compelled to believe and were not granted respite.

It is possible that what is meant is the judgment (القضاء) between the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the nation, which will occur at the end of time, as previously hinted at in the commentary on His saying: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ** (For every nation there is an apostle) [Yunus: 47].

In any case, what is meant by the two verses is the polytheists' seeking to hasten the victory, and the answer is that it is a victory in which the faith of those who disbelieve will not benefit them, because it is a time when faith will not benefit a soul, nor will the punishment be delayed or grant them respite.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَانْتَظِرْ إِنَّهُمْ مُنْتَظِرُونَ** — "So turn away from them and wait; they too are waiting" (32:30). A command to turn away from them and await the victory, just as they are waiting. They were waiting for his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) death or killing — in short, the cessation of his true call. So let him wait as they wait, until Allah makes the truth prevail over falsehood and the one on the truth over the one on falsehood.

From this context, it appears that what is meant by "the victory" (الفتح) is the worldly victory.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said regarding: **تَتَجافى جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ** (Their sides vacate their beds): "They are those who do not sleep before the night prayer (العشاء)." So he praised them — and when that was mentioned, a man began to avoid his bed for fear that his eye would overcome him — so its time is before the young child sleeps and the elder becomes weary.

**I say**: It was also narrated therein through other paths, both connected (موصولة) and halted (موقوفة). Al-Shaykh narrated the beginning of the hadith in his *Amali* with a chain to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) regarding the verse, and its wording is: "They used to not sleep until they prayed the late night prayer (العتمة)."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Sulayman ibn Khalid from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Shall I not inform you of Islam — its root, its branch, and the peak of its hump?" I said: "Yes, may I be your ransom." He said: "As for its root, it is prayer (الصلاة); its branch is the alms-tax (الزكاة); and the peak of its hump is struggle (الجهاد)."

Then he said: "If you wish, I shall inform you of the gates of goodness." I said: "Yes, may I be your ransom." He said: "Fasting is a shield, and charity removes sin — and a man's standing in the depth of the night remembering Allah." Then he recited: **تَتَجافى جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ** (Their sides vacate their beds).

**I say**: This meaning was narrated in *al-Mahasin* with its chain to Ali ibn Abd al-Aziz from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام). In *al-Majma‘*, from al-Wahidi with a chain to Mu'adh ibn Jabal from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, and others from Mu'adh from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir narrated from Mujahid who said: "The Messenger of Allah mentioned the night prayer (قيام الليل) and his eyes overflowed — until his tears poured down — and he said: **تَتَجافى جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ** (Their sides vacate their beds)."

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, Muslim, al-Tabarani, Ibn Jarir, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and Muhammad ibn Nasr in the *Book of Prayer* narrated via Abu Sakhr from Abu Hazim from Sahl ibn Sa'd who said: "While we were with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and he was describing Paradise until he finished.

Then he said: 'In it is what no eye has seen — nor ear has heard, nor has it occurred to the heart of a human.' Then he recited: **تَتَجافى جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ** (Their sides vacate their beds) — the two verses."

In *al-Majma‘*, it is narrated from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) that he said: "There is no good deed (حسنة) except that it has a clear reward in the Quran, except for the night prayer (صلاة الليل) — for Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), did not clarify its reward due to the greatness of its significance (عظم خطرها). He said: **فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ** (No soul knows) — the verse."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, my father narrated from Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Najran from Asim ibn Humayd from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "There is no good deed a servant performs — except that it has a reward in the Quran, except for the night prayer — for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, did not clarify its reward due to the greatness of its significance with Him. So He, exalted is His mention (جل ذكره), said: **تَتَجافى جُنُوبُهُمْ عَنِ الْمَضاجِعِ ـ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً وَمِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ** — until His saying — **يَعْمَلُونَ**. Then he said: 'Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has a bestowal of honor (كرامة) for His believing servants — on every Friday. When it is Friday — Allah sends to the believer an angel with two robes, who reaches the gate of Paradise — and says: "Seek permission for me to enter upon so-and-so." It is said to him: "This is the messenger of your Lord at the gate." He says to his spouses: "What do you see on me that is most beautiful?" They say: "O our master, by Him who made Paradise permissible for you — we have not seen anything on you more beautiful than this which your Lord has sent to you." So he wears one as a waist-wrap and the other as a cloak — and he does not pass by anything except that it lights up for him until he reaches the appointed place.'

When they gather, the Lord, Blessed and Exalted is He, manifests Himself to them — and when they look at Him, meaning at His mercy, they fall down in prostration — so He says: 'My servants, raise your heads, for here is not a day of prostration — nor of worship; the burden (المئونة) has been lifted from you.' They say: 'O our Lord, what thing is better than what You have given us? You have given us Paradise.' He says: 'For you is the like of what is in your hands seventy times over.'

So the believer returns every Friday with seventy times the like of what was in his hands — and that is His saying: **وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ** (and with Us there is more) [50:35], and it is Friday — its night is a brilliant night and its day is a radiant day — so increase in glorification (التسبيح), declaration of oneness (التهليل), magnification (التكبير) — and praise of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and blessings upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).'

He said: 'The believer passes by, and he does not pass by anything except that it lights up for him — until he reaches his spouses, and they say: "By Him who made Paradise permissible for us, O our master, we have not seen you more beautiful than you are this hour." He says: "I looked at the light of my Lord" — until he said — I said: "May I be your ransom, tell me more." He said: "Indeed, Allah the Exalted created a garden with His hand — no eye has seen it and no creature has looked upon it — the Lord opens it every morning and says: 'Increase in fragrance, increase in pleasantness.' And that is the saying of Allah: **فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ ـ جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** (No soul knows what has been kept hidden for them of bright eyes as a reward for what they used to do).'"

**I say**: The end of the narration is an explanation of its beginning, and his saying: "meaning at the mercy of his Lord" is from the speech of the narrator.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Maymun al-Qaddah from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "Whoever feeds a believer until he is satiated — no one from the creation of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, knows what reward he has — in the Hereafter, neither a proximate angel nor a sent prophet, except Allah, the Lord of all the worlds."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ مُؤْمِناً كَمَنْ كانَ فاسِقاً لا يَسْتَوُونَ** (Is he who is a believer like him who is a transgressor? They are not equal): He said: "Indeed, Ali ibn Abi Talib and al-Walid ibn Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt quarreled — so the transgressor (الفاسق) Walid ibn Uqbah said: 'By Allah, I am more eloquent of tongue than you, sharper of spearhead than you — and more steadfast in the battalion than you.' Ali (عليه‌السلام) said: 'Be silent, for you are only a transgressor (فاسق).' So Allah revealed: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ مُؤْمِناً كَمَنْ كانَ فاسِقاً لا يَسْتَوُونَ**."

**I say**: It was narrated in *al-Majma‘* from al-Wahidi from Ibn Abbas. In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from the *Book of Songs* (الأغاني), al-Wahidi, Ibn Adi, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Khatib, and Ibn Asakir from him; also from Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Jarir from Ata ibn Yasar; and from Ibn Abi Hatim from al-Suddi from him; and also from Ibn Abi Hatim from Ibn Abi Layla similarly.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from al-Hasan ibn Ali (عليه‌السلام) in a tradition where he argues with men in the presence of Mu'awiyah: "As for you, O Walid ibn Uqbah — by Allah, I do not blame you for hating Ali — for he lashed you eighty times for [drinking] wine — and killed your father in captivity with his own hand on the Day of Badr. How can you revile him — when Allah has named him a believer in ten verses of the Quran — and named you a transgressor (فاسقاً)? And that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ مُؤْمِناً كَمَنْ كانَ فاسِقاً لا يَسْتَوُونَ**."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Idris al-Khawlani who said: "I asked Ubadah ibn al-Samit about the saying of Allah: **وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُمْ مِنَ الْعَذابِ الْأَدْنى دُونَ الْعَذابِ الْأَكْبَرِ** (We shall surely make them taste the nearer punishment prior to the greater punishment). He said: 'I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about it and he said: "It refers to calamities, illnesses, and hardships — a punishment for the extravagant in this world prior to the punishment of the Hereafter." I said: "O Messenger of Allah, what is it for us?" He said: "A purification (زكاة) and a cleansing (طهور)."'"

In *al-Majma‘*, in the narration from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): "The nearer punishment (العذاب الأدنى) is the Beast (الدابة) and the Antichrist (الدجال)."
